docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Welcome to the VG Language Documentation! 🎉

VG Language is a lightweight, expressive programming language designed for simplicity and ease of use. Whether you're a beginner or an experienced developer, VG Language provides an intuitive syntax for efficient coding.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Simple and readable syntax - Easy to learn and write.
  • Support for variables, constants, and arrays - Store and manipulate data effortlessly.
  • Control flow with if statements and loops - Make decisions and repeat tasks efficiently.
  • Built-in print functionality – Quickly display output for debugging and results.
  • Commenting system – Add notes to your code for clarity and documentation.
  • Functions – Define reusable blocks of code to organize and simplify your programs.

docs.title: Get Started Now! ⬇️
docs.text: Click the button below to download the latest version of VG Language and start coding today!

docs.title: Arrays in VG Language
docs.text: Arrays in VG Language store multiple values in a single variable. They can hold different data types, and they support both one-dimensional and nested arrays.

docs.title: One-Dimensional Arrays
docs.text:
A simple array stores elements of any type:

var numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
print(numbers[2]); // Output: 3

var names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"];
print(names[0]); // Output: Alice

var mixed = [10, "Hello", true, 3.14];
print(mixed[1]); // Output: Hello

docs.title: Nested Arrays
docs.text:
var matrix = [
    [1, 2, 3],
    [4, 5, 6],
    [7, 8, 9]
];

print(matrix[1][2]); // Output: 6

var complexArray = [
    ["Alice", 25, true],
    ["Bob", 30, false]
];

print(complexArray[0][0]); // Output: Alice
print(complexArray[1][1]); // Output: 30

docs.title: Modifying Arrays
docs.text:
var items = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"];
items[1] = "blueberry";

print(items); ## Output: ["apple", "blueberry", "cherry"]

docs.title: VG Language Commands
docs.text: VG Language provides a command-line interface (CLI) for running .vg scripts.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
To execute a VG script, use:

vg <file.vg> [options]

docs.title: Help Command
docs.text:
To display the help command, use:

vg --help
that would display the following: 
Usage: vg <file.vg> [options]
Options:
  --help        Show this help menu
  <file.vg>     The VG source file to interpret

docs.title: Comments in VG Language
docs.text: Comments are used to add explanatory notes or disable parts of the code. VG Language supports single-line and multi-line comments.

docs.title: Single-Line Comments
docs.text:
A single-line comment begins with ## and continues to the end of the line:

## This is a single-line comment
var x = 10;  ## Variable declaration

/# This is a
multi-line comment
spanning multiple lines
#/

var y = 20;

docs.title: Constants in VG Language
docs.text: Constants are similar to variables, but their values cannot be changed once they are assigned. You declare a constant using the const keyword.

docs.title: Declaring Constants
docs.text:
To declare a constant, use the const keyword followed by the name and value:

const PI = 3.14159;
const MAX_VALUE = 100;

docs.title: Constant Types
docs.text:
Like variables, constants can have various data types:

  • Integers: Whole numbers (e.g., 100, -50)
  • Doubles: Decimal numbers (e.g., 3.14, 2.71)
  • Strings: Text (e.g., "Hello, world!")
  • Booleans: true or false 
    const greeting = "Hello, VG!"; ## String constant
const maxAge = 100;            ## Integer constant

docs.title: Why Use Constants?
docs.text:
• Constants are useful when you need a value that should remain unchanged throughout the program (e.g., mathematical constants like PI, or configuration values like MAX_VALUE).
  • Declaring values as constants helps to prevent accidental changes and makes the code more readable.

docs.title: Expressions in VG Language
docs.text:
Expressions form the building blocks of VG Language, including: - Arithmetic operations (+, -, *, /, %) - Logical operations (&&, ||, !) - Comparisons (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=)

Example: 

var result = (10 + 5) * 2;
print(result);  ## Output: 30

docs.title: Installing VG Language
docs.text: VG Language is easy to install on Windows. Follow the steps below to set up VG Language on your system.

docs.title: Windows Installation 🖥️
docs.text:
1. Download the VG Language installer

2. Run the installer (VGSetup.exe) and follow the on-screen instructions.

If installed correctly, it will display the VG Language version. 3. Run a VG script: sh vg my_script.vg This executes my_script.vg in the VG Language interpreter

docs.title: overview
docs.text:
VG Language is designed for both beginners and experienced programmers. This guide will help you get started with the syntax, features, and usage. with the syntax, features, and usage.

VG Language aims to provide a simple yet powerful syntax that enables developers to write clean and efficient code. Whether you are new to programming or transitioning from another language, this guide will cover the fundamental concepts you need to know.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Simple Syntax – Easy-to-read structure with minimal complexity.
  • Dynamic Typing – Supports flexible variable assignments.
  • Control Structures – Includes loops, conditional statements, and functions.
  • Data Structures – Offers arrays, dictionaries, and custom objects.
  • Modular Design – Encourages reusable and maintainable code.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
1. Installation: Download and install the VG Language compiler or interpreter.
  2. Writing Code: Create a new file with the .vg extension and start coding.
  3. Running Code: Use the VG Language interpreter or compiler to execute your script.

Example: 

print("Hello, World!");  ##Output: Hello, World!

docs.title: Statements
docs.text:
VG Language consists of statements, which include:

  • Variable declarations
  • Constants
  • Arrays
  • Assignments
  • Print statements
  • Conditional statements
  • Loops
  • Functions
  • Try-Catch statements

docs.title: Variables in VG Language
docs.text: Variables are used to store data values. In VG Language, you can declare variables and assign them values.

docs.title: Declaring Variables
docs.text:
To declare a variable, use the var keyword:

var x = 10;
var name = "Alice";
var isActive = true;

docs.title: Variable Types
docs.text:
VG Language supports various data types for variables:

  • Integers: Whole numbers (e.g., 10, -5)
  • Doubles: Decimal numbers (e.g., 3.14, -0.99)
  • Strings: Text enclosed in double quotes (e.g., "Hello, world!")
  • Booleans: true or false
  • Arrays: A list of elements (e.g., [1, 2, 3], ["a", "b", "c"])

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
var age = 25;         ## Integer
var temperature = 23.5;  ## Double
var greeting = "Hello, VG!"; ## String
var isActive = true;   ## Boolean

docs.title: Updating Variables
docs.text:
var x = 5;
x = x + 3;  ## x is now 8
print(x);  ## Output: 8

docs.title: Do-While Loop in VG Language
docs.text:
The do-while loop in VG Language executes a block of code at least once, regardless of the condition. It checks the condition after executing the loop body, making it different from a regular while loop.

var x = 0;
do {
    print(x);
    x = x + 1;
} while (x < 5);

docs.title: For Loop in VG Language
docs.text:
A for loop is used when you know in advance how many times you want to execute a block of code. It consists of:

  1. Initialization – A variable is initialized before the loop starts.
  2. Condition – The loop runs as long as this condition is true.
  3. Update – The variable is updated after each iteration.

docs.title: Basic Syntax
docs.text:
for (var i = 0; i < 5; i = i + 1) {
    print(i);
}

docs.title: If Statements in VG Language
docs.text:
Used to execute code conditionally.

if (x > 10) {
    print("x is greater than 10");
} else {
    print("x is 10 or less");
}

docs.title: While Loop in VG Language
docs.text:
A while loop repeatedly executes a block of code as long as a given condition evaluates to true. This is useful when the number of iterations is not known beforehand.

var x = 0;
while (x < 5) {
    print(x);
    x = x + 1;
}
